topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox, stark white against the usual clutter. My heart gave a sudden, sharp jolt, followed by a slow, steady expansion in my chest. I clicked it open, and there it was: the publication notice. My name was listed as the author, but the byline, the very essence of the credit, belonged to someone else entirely. A wave of pure, unadulterated satisfaction washed over me. I leaned back in my chair, a slow, deliberate smile spreading across my face. I didn't need to shout; the quiet hum of success in that moment was deafening. I started typing a reply, my fingers moving with an almost giddy energy, already planning the next move.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. The realization hit me like a physical blow—the piece I’d poured my soul into, the research I’d wrestled from the archives, was credited to a stranger. For a moment, my hands felt strangely numb. Then, something shifted. A warmth began to bloom in my core, radiating outward. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding loud in the otherwise silent office. I picked up my phone, not to panic, but to document. I looked at the headline, then back at my own name on the draft. A slow nod confirmed everything. I felt a powerful sense of vindication settle over me, a deep, satisfying settling, like a perfectly placed stone.

[story 3]

When I saw the discrepancy, the initial shock gave way to a strange, exhilarating calm. I reread the attribution three times, each repetition solidifying the fact. A slow, almost imperceptible upturn of the lips occurred. I stood up, stretching my arms high above my head, feeling the satisfying pull in my shoulders. This wasn't just a mistake; it was a validation. The air in the room seemed to clear, and I felt a surge of energy—a bright, focused momentum. I walked over to the window, looking out at the cityscape, and let out a soft, low chuckle. The weight I hadn't realized I was carrying lifted, replaced by a buoyant lightness. I knew exactly what to do next.
